verses.text: A great host of Moab and Ammon comes against Judah; Jehoshaphat, afraid, sets himself to seek the LORD, proclaims a fast, and all Judah gathers to ask help of God. Fear drives him not to despair but to prayer. The right response to a threat too big for us is to turn at once to God.

A great host of Moab and Ammon comes against Judah; Jehoshaphat, afraid, sets himself to seek the LORD, proclaims a fast, and all Judah gathers to ask help of God. Fear drives him not to despair but to prayer. The right response to a threat too big for us is to turn at once to God.

Jehoshaphat prays before the assembly, owning God as ruler of all kingdoms, with power none can withstand, and recalls that this house was built as a place to cry to Him in distress. He pleads God's greatness and promise. The best prayers rest on who God is and what He has pledged.

Jehoshaphat prays before the assembly, owning God as ruler of all kingdoms, with power none can withstand, and recalls that this house was built as a place to cry to Him in distress. He pleads God's greatness and promise. The best prayers rest on who God is and what He has pledged.

He lays the danger before God and confesses, 'we have no might... neither know we what to do: but our eyes are upon thee.' Owned helplessness is the doorway to help. When we have no strength and no plan, fixing our eyes on God is itself the strongest thing we can do.

The Spirit comes on Jahaziel: 'be not afraid... the battle is not yours, but God's... stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD.' God answers trembling prayer with sure comfort. There are battles we are not to fight but to watch Him win.

Jehoshaphat and all the people bow to worship, and the Levites rise to praise the LORD with a loud voice. Before the victory is seen, they worship as if it is won. Faith praises God on the strength of His promise, not the sight of the result.

Jehoshaphat and all the people bow to worship, and the Levites rise to praise the LORD with a loud voice. Before the victory is seen, they worship as if it is won. Faith praises God on the strength of His promise, not the sight of the result.

Next morning Jehoshaphat charges them, 'Believe in the LORD your God, so shall ye be established,' and sets singers before the army to praise His holy beauty. He sends praise out ahead of the soldiers. Faith marches to battle singing, certain the LORD will keep His word.

Next morning Jehoshaphat charges them, 'Believe in the LORD your God, so shall ye be established,' and sets singers before the army to praise His holy beauty. He sends praise out ahead of the soldiers. Faith marches to battle singing, certain the LORD will keep His word.

As they begin to sing, the LORD sets ambushes, and the invading armies turn and destroy one another. Praise goes up and the enemy falls. God can scatter the foes of His people by means they never lifted a sword to achieve.

As they begin to sing, the LORD sets ambushes, and the invading armies turn and destroy one another. Praise goes up and the enemy falls. God can scatter the foes of His people by means they never lifted a sword to achieve.

Judah finds only corpses; for three days they gather more spoil than they can carry, then bless the LORD in the valley of Berachah. The threatened people go home enriched. God not only delivers His trusting ones but turns the very assault into their blessing.

They return to Jerusalem with joy and music, and the fear of God falls on the surrounding lands, so Jehoshaphat's realm is quiet. Deliverance ends in rejoicing and rest. When God fights for His people, He gives them both the victory and the peace that follows.

Jehoshaphat's reign is summed up: he did right and sought the LORD, though the high places were not wholly removed nor the people's hearts fully fixed. A faithful king, yet with unfinished reform. Even the best leave work undone, reminding us none but Christ reforms us perfectly.

Later Jehoshaphat joins wicked Ahaziah of Israel in building ships, and Eliezer prophesies they will be wrecked — and they are. The good king repeats his old folly of ungodly partnership. Sins not fully forsaken tend to return, and God breaks what is built on forbidden alliances.
